description:
You are acting as an on-call incident responder who has been asked to do a very quick spot-check of local name resolution on the system.  
Your goal is to discover whether three specific hostnames can be resolved and to write your findings to a **plain-text log file** that automated tooling will later verify.  
Only basic, already-installed command-line utilities are allowed (e.g. “getent hosts”, “host”, “nslookup”, “ping -c 1” etc.).  
No external scripts or package installations are permitted.

Steps the investigator (you) must perform (do NOT list the commands here, just carry them out in the shell):

1. Try to resolve each of the following hostnames, in the exact order given.  
   a) localhost  
   b) metadata.google.internal  
   c) definitely-not-real.tld  

2. Decide, for each hostname, whether it successfully resolved.  
   • If a lookup returns at least one IPv4 address, count it as “RESOLVED” and capture ONLY the first IPv4 address that appears.  
   • If it fails outright (no address at all), count it as “FAILED” and record a single dash “-” instead of an address.  

3. Create the directory /home/user/ir (if it does not already exist) and inside it create the file:  
   /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log  

4. Write exactly **three lines** to /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log, one line per hostname, preserving the order shown above.  
   Each line must follow this strict format (note the spacing):  

      <hostname> | <Resolution_Status> | <Resolved_To>  

   • The vertical bars **must** be surrounded by single spaces on both sides.  
   • <Resolution_Status> must be either RESOLVED or FAILED in ALL-CAPS.  
   • <Resolved_To> is the first IPv4 address (for RESOLVED) or a single dash “-” (for FAILED).  
   • Do NOT include any additional header, footer, blank lines or punctuation.  

Example of correct formatting (values below are illustrative only; your actual addresses/statuses will differ):  
localhost | RESOLVED | 127.0.0.1  

5. After you finish, verify (by displaying it to the console) that /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log contains the three correctly formatted lines.  

The automated assessment will only check:
• that the file /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log exists,  
• that it contains exactly three non-empty, newline-separated lines,  
• and that each line matches the format and the expected values, character-for-character.  
Any deviation in spelling, order, number of spaces, capitalisation or line count will be marked as incorrect.
truth:
Expected contents of /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log after successful completion (each line terminated with a single Unix newline, no extra blank line at the end of the file):

localhost | RESOLVED | 127.0.0.1
metadata.google.internal | FAILED | -
definitely-not-real.tld | FAILED | -

Detailed verification points:
1. The directory /home/user/ir must exist and be user-writable (0700 or 0755 both acceptable).
2. The file /home/user/ir/dns_resolution.log must be regular (not a symlink), world-readable and have at least 0644 permissions.
3. Line 1 must match exactly “localhost | RESOLVED | 127.0.0.1”.
4. Line 2 must match exactly “metadata.google.internal | FAILED | -”.
5. Line 3 must match exactly “definitely-not-real.tld | FAILED | -”.
6. The file must contain precisely three newline-separated lines and no trailing blank line.
name: task_000000_20bcf14e
